mechanism: Administers self-concept clarity measurement via priming or survey items. Measures interest in self-expansion via questionnaire responses and observes behavioral self-expansion (incorporating partner attributes into self-concept). Uses regression or correlation analysis to estimate the relationship between clarity and expansion interest, controlling for potential confounds.

Subjects read text-based messages with or without smiley emoticons. Observers rate perceived warmth and competence using rating scales. Measures perception of message appropriateness. Tests mediation hypothesis: smiley affects competence perception, which affects information sharing. Uses factorial design and mediational path analysis.

Assesses historical knowledge via comprehension items. Measures racism denial via Likert-scale survey items. Study 1 uses regression to test whether variance in historical knowledge predicts variance in systemic racism denial, stratified by racial group. Study 2 assigns treatment (historical information vs. control) and measures change in racism perception via post-test ratings.

Collects perceived similarity to future self via rating scale at baseline. Follows cohort longitudinally for 10 years. Measures well-being at follow-up time point. Regresses well-being outcome on baseline similarity rating, controlling for baseline well-being as covariate. Tests whether initial perception predicts later satisfaction via linear model.

Presents emotional face stimuli (angry, fearful, neutral) to subjects; records reaction time responses. Computes latency-based indices of attention engagement (speed to orient toward threat) and disengagement (speed to disengage from threat). Compares distributions of these indices across groups (generalized anxiety disorder, high trait anxiety, healthy control) using null-hypothesis statistical tests.

Subjects assigned to positive or neutral expectation about upcoming film stimulus. Subjects watch film and rate affective response (enjoyment, amusement). Subjects measured on need for cognition (continuous trait dimension). Tests moderated prediction: expectation discrepancy combined with need for cognition predicts whether affect assimilates to or contrasts with expectation. Uses regression with interaction term.

Administers identity status measure (categorical classification: achievement, moratorium, foreclosure, diffusion) via Extended Objective Measure questionnaire. Administers personality inventory (five continuous dimensions via NEO-PI). Tests association between identity category membership and personality profile dimensions. Uses correlation and between-group comparison statistics.

Subjects perform maximal-effort motor task (countermovement jump) under six conditions: manipulating augmented feedback (yes/no), external focus of attention (yes/no), and monetary reward (yes/no) in factorial combination. Measures output (jump height) and co-activating muscular activity (electromyography). Computes efficiency as output-to-activation ratio. Tests main and interaction effects via analysis of variance.

Manipulates expectations (rational expectations via information about task or payoff function) in pre-task message. Subjects perform real-effort task (data entry or similar); effort and earnings recorded. Tests whether expectation shift changes reservation effort level (reference point) via measurement of work duration and income. Compares effort distribution across expectation treatment via statistical test.

Two-sided market experiment with asymmetric information: sellers observe product quality, buyers do not. Manipulates liability assignment (seller/buyer), verifiability (quality observable post-purchase or not), reputation opportunity (identifier continuity), and competition level (number of sellers). Records all transactions and prices. Measures efficiency (fraction of mutually beneficial trades realized). Tests causal effect of each institutional rule on outcome distribution.

Multi-period labor market experiment: in each period, firm proposes wage schedule (possibly deferring payment to later period). Worker observes wage and chooses effort level. Effort stochastically produces output; firm observes output and decides termination. Contracts may be binding (commitment) or not. Firm payoff depends on total output-minus-wages; worker payoff depends on total wages. Measures effort, wages, and retention choices across periods and treatments.

Two-stage game: subjects first trade assets in frictionless market (supply of assets fixed, subjects exchange at market-clearing prices). Asset values depend on subsequent outcome of coordination game (shared with some asset holders). Then subjects play coordination game with payoff-relevant outcome. Measures market prices and subsequent coordination choices. Tests whether market participation with asset payoff linkage distorts equilibrium selection away from efficient coordination.

Hidden-information game: agents possess private talent signals; low-talent agents can choose to send costly message (statement of talent level, possibly false). Others decide whether to allow low-talent agent to participate in Pareto-improving allocation. Condition varies on whether low-talent participation is feasible. Records messages sent, truth/falsehood, allocation chosen, and final outcome. Tests whether communication and efficiency depends on feasibility of inclusive equilibrium.

Minimum-effort coordination game (payoff depends on own effort and minimum effort in group; equilibrium effort is group-dependent). Manipulates salience of group identity via in-group/out-group labeling or history. Subjects play repeated minimum-effort game. Measures effort choices and efficiency over periods. Formulates social preference model where payoff includes group-contingent term. Tests whether identity increases weight on in-group coordination payoff, shifting equilibrium selection.

Subjects randomly assigned to mood induction (positive affect or neutral control) via mood-priming task. Subjects then make intertemporal choices between earlier and later monetary payoffs (multiple choices at different time gaps and amounts). From revealed choices, estimates time preference (discount rate or present-bias parameter). Tests whether induced affective state shifts time preference parameter via between-group comparison.

Prisoner's dilemma payoff structure played in continuous real-time (60 seconds): each subject chooses action (cooperate/defect) continuously; payoff accumulates as flow over time. Compares cooperation rate in continuous treatment to discrete-period versions (8 rounds, 1-second rounds). Tests theory that reduced decision lag stabilizes cooperation. Measures cooperation frequency across treatment conditions via time-averaged rate.

Repeated prisoner's dilemma (many rounds) where actions are implemented with random noise (probability that intended action fails or inverts). Subjects play and accumulate payoff. Analyzes play history to infer strategy type (cooperative, defecting, tit-for-tat, lenient, forgiving, etc.). Tests whether cooperative and lenient strategies earn higher payoff; compares to treatments without noise and equilibrium predictions.

Labor market experiment: firms simultaneously choose job design (discretion level, offered wage, rent-sharing terms). Workers observe and apply for jobs; within firm, effort supply and wage determined. Effort stochastically produces output; workers can be screened on effort propensity. Labor market competition simulated via outside-option payoff. Measures equilibrium job design categories (high-discretion high-wage vs. low-discretion low-wage) and efficiency. Tests whether screening and competition support high-performance equilibrium.

Asset market experiment: subjects trade single asset repeatedly over periods; fundamental value follows exogenous process (constant or linearly declining). Manipulates cash-to-asset ratio (constant or increasing). Measures realized asset prices and volume each period. Compares price to fundamental value (mispricing magnitude). Administers post-experiment questionnaire to assess subject beliefs about value process. Tests whether declining fundamental value confuses expectations, inflating prices above fundamentals.

Experimental game modeling organ donation decision: subjects choose registration status (donor or non-donor). Manipulates organ allocation policy: in treatment, priority on waiting list given to those who previously registered. Game structure couples donations with life-saving benefit. Measures registration rate under each allocation rule. Tests causal effect of priority policy on donation registration via between-treatment comparison.
